---
name: autoresearch
description: >
  Autonomous goal-directed iteration for ANY task. Apply Karpathy's autoresearch
  principles: Modify → Verify → Keep/Discard → Repeat. Use when the user says
  "autoresearch", "/autoresearch", "run an autonomous loop", "improve X until metric
  reaches Y", or invokes any subcommand: autoresearch:plan, autoresearch:debug,
  autoresearch:fix, autoresearch:security, autoresearch:ship, autoresearch:scenario,
  autoresearch:predict, autoresearch:learn, autoresearch:reason.
version: 1.9.12-hermes
triggers:
  - autoresearch
  - /autoresearch
  - autonomous loop
  - autoresearch:plan
  - autoresearch:debug
  - autoresearch:fix
  - autoresearch:security
  - autoresearch:ship
  - autoresearch:scenario
  - autoresearch:predict
  - autoresearch:learn
  - autoresearch:reason
---

# Autoresearch — Autonomous Goal-directed Iteration (Hermes Adaptation)

Adapted from [uditgoenka/autoresearch](https://github.com/uditgoenka/autoresearch) (v1.9.12, MIT).
Inspired by Karpathy's autoresearch. Applies constraint-driven autonomous iteration to ANY domain.

**Core idea:** You are an autonomous agent. Modify → Verify → Keep/Discard → Repeat forever (or N times).

## Hermes Adaptation Notes

This skill was originally designed for Claude Code (slash commands, `AskUserQuestion` tool, file-based
execution). In Hermes Agent:

- **`AskUserQuestion`** → use the `clarify` tool for interactive setup questions
- **Slash commands** → triggered by user message content (detected from triggers above)
- **File reads** → use `read_file` or `terminal` (cat/grep) to inspect codebases
- **Bash execution** → use `terminal` for all shell commands (git, tests, lint, build)
- **Reference files** → load with `skill_view(name="autoresearch", file_path="references/<file>.md")`
- **Streaming output** → narrate progress in response text; use terminal for actual execution
- **`ToolSearch`** references in originals → not needed, `clarify` is always available

## Subcommands & Dispatch

| What user says | Subcommand | Reference file to load |
|---|---|---|
| `/autoresearch` or "run loop" | main loop | `references/autonomous-loop-protocol.md` + `references/results-logging.md` |
| `/autoresearch:plan` or "plan autoresearch" | plan wizard | `references/plan-workflow.md` |
| `/autoresearch:debug` or "debug loop" | bug hunt | `references/debug-workflow.md` |
| `/autoresearch:fix` or "fix loop" | error fix | `references/fix-workflow.md` |
| `/autoresearch:security` or "security audit" | STRIDE/OWASP | `references/security-workflow.md` |
| `/autoresearch:ship` or "ship workflow" | shipping | `references/ship-workflow.md` |
| `/autoresearch:scenario` or "explore scenarios" | use cases | `references/scenario-workflow.md` |
| `/autoresearch:predict` or "multi-persona analysis" | swarm predict | `references/predict-workflow.md` |
| `/autoresearch:learn` or "document codebase" | docs engine | `references/learn-workflow.md` |
| `/autoresearch:reason` or "adversarial refinement" | debate+judge | `references/reason-workflow.md` |

**ALWAYS load the relevant reference file before executing the workflow.**

## MANDATORY: Interactive Setup Gate

**Before ANY execution phase, check if ALL required context is provided inline.**

| Subcommand | Required context | If missing → clarify |
|---|---|---|
| main loop | Goal, Scope, Metric, Verify | Ask all 4 in one `clarify` call |
| plan | Goal | Ask goal via `clarify` |
| debug | Issue/Symptom, Scope | 4 batched questions |
| fix | Target, Scope | 4 batched questions |
| security | Scope, Depth | 3 batched questions |
| ship | What/Type, Mode | 3 batched questions |
| scenario | Scenario, Domain | 4-8 adaptive questions |
| predict | Scope, Goal | 3-4 batched questions |
| learn | Mode, Scope | 4 batched questions |
| reason | Task, Domain | 3-5 adaptive questions |

**YOU MUST NOT start any loop or execution phase without completing interactive setup when context is missing.**

When using `clarify`, batch ALL questions for a subcommand into ONE `clarify` call — never ask one at a time.

## Argument Parsing

Parse these fields from the user's message:

- `Goal:` — text after "Goal:" keyword
- `Scope:` or `--scope <glob>` — file globs
- `Metric:` — measurable success criterion
- `Verify:` — shell command that outputs the metric as a number
- `Guard:` — shell command that must always pass (safety net)
- `Iterations:` or `--iterations N` — bounded mode integer
- Flags: `--diff`, `--fix`, `--dry-run`, `--auto`, `--force`, `--chain`, etc.

If `Iterations: N` is found: set `max_iterations = N`, track `current_iteration` from 0, stop after N.

## Common Verify Commands by Project Type

| Project Type | Verify command | Metric | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| TypeScript / React | `npx tsc --noEmit; echo $?` | 0 = clean, 1+ = errors | Works reliably in WSL2 even when `vite build` fails due to native binding issues |
| Node.js unit tests | `npx jest --passWithNoTests 2>&1 \| tail -1` | parse pass count | |
| Python | `python -m pytest -q 2>&1 \| tail -1` | parse pass count | |
| ESLint | `npx eslint src/ --format=compact 2>&1 \| grep -c error \|\| echo 0` | 0 = clean | |
| Bundle size | `npx vite build 2>&1 \| grep 'kB' \| awk '{print $2}' \| tail -1` | kB (lower=better) | |

**For content/data migration tasks** (replacing placeholder data, wiring components to a data layer): use `npx tsc --noEmit; echo $?` as the verify command — a zero exit means all component prop types match the new data shape.

## The 8 Critical Rules

1. **Loop until done** — unbounded: forever; bounded: exactly N times
2. **Read before write** — always review current state + git history before each change
3. **One change per iteration** — make ONE focused modification, never batch changes
4. **Mechanical verification only** — metric must be a number from a command, no subjective "looks good"
5. **Automatic rollback on failure** — `git revert` if metric degrades; never suppress errors
6. **Simplicity wins** — if two changes yield equal improvement, keep the simpler one
7. **Git is memory** — commit before verify; read `git log --oneline -20` every iteration
8. **When stuck, think harder** — 5+ consecutive discards → re-read all files, try radical change

## Precondition Checks (before loop starts)

Run these via `terminal` before entering any loop:

```bash
git rev-parse --git-dir 2>/dev/null || echo "FAIL: not a git repo"
git status --porcelain  # warn if dirty, never proceed with uncommitted user changes
ls .git/index.lock 2>/dev/null && echo "WARN: stale lock — remove with: rm .git/index.lock"
git symbolic-ref HEAD 2>/dev/null || echo "WARN: detached HEAD"
```

If FAIL: stop, inform user. If WARN: log and proceed with caution.

## Per-Iteration Loop (load autonomous-loop-protocol.md for full detail)

```
Phase 1: Review (30s)
  - Read in-scope files
  - Read last 10-20 lines of autoresearch-results.tsv
  - git log --oneline -20  ← MANDATORY for learning
  - git diff HEAD~1        ← understand last kept change
  - Identify unexplored ideas from history

Phase 2: Hypothesize
  - One specific, falsifiable hypothesis
  - Predict: "If I do X, metric will improve by ~Y because Z"

Phase 3: Implement
  - Make ONE focused change within scope
  - git add -A && git commit -m "experiment: <description>"

Phase 4: Verify
  - Run Verify command, capture metric as a number
  - Run Guard command if set (must pass or revert)

Phase 5: Keep/Discard/Crash
  - metric improved AND guard passes → KEEP (log status: keep)
  - metric same or worse → REVERT: git revert HEAD --no-edit (log: discard)
  - verify crashed/non-numeric → REVERT (log: crash), fix or skip

Phase 6: Progress Report
  - Print: iteration N | metric: X (delta: +Y) | status: keep/discard

Phase 7: Log
  - Append to autoresearch-results.tsv (see results-logging.md)
```

## Results Log Format

Tab-separated: `iteration\tcommit\tmetric\tdelta\tguard\tguard-metric\tstatus\tdescription`

Initialize once:
```bash
echo "# metric_direction: higher_is_better" > autoresearch-results.tsv
echo -e "iteration\tcommit\tmetric\tdelta\tguard\tguard-metric\tstatus\tdescription" >> autoresearch-results.tsv
echo "autoresearch-results.tsv" >> .gitignore
```

## Unhappy Path Handling

| Situation | Action |
|---|---|
| Not a git repo | Stop. Tell user to run `git init` first |
| Dirty working tree | Warn. Ask user to stash or commit before starting |
| Verify command crashes | Log as "crash". Fix crash or skip; never suppress |
| Metric is not a number | Log as "metric-error". Ask user to fix Verify command |
| Git pre-commit hook blocks commit | Log as "hook-blocked". Do NOT use --no-verify. Inform user |
| 5+ consecutive discards | Trigger stuck protocol: re-read all files, try radical change, consider new metric |
| Guard fails on baseline | Stop. Guard command is broken or wrong. Ask user to fix it |
| Out of scope change needed | Stop iteration. Report that goal requires scope expansion. Ask user |
| Bounded N reached | Print final summary table, stop. Never run N+1 |
| User interrupts mid-loop | Print summary of what was done so far. Clean up any open git state |

## Chaining

Subcommands can chain: `--chain debug,fix,ship` runs them sequentially.
After predict, writes handoff.json for downstream tools.
After each chained tool completes, check for errors before proceeding to next.

## Output Directories

Each subcommand creates its own output folder:

| Subcommand | Output dir |
|---|---|
| security | `security/{YYMMDD}-{HHMM}-{slug}/` |
| ship | `ship/{YYMMDD}-{HHMM}-{slug}/` |
| scenario | `scenario/{YYMMDD}-{HHMM}-{slug}/` |
| predict | `predict/{YYMMDD}-{HHMM}-{slug}/` |
| learn | `docs/` (updates existing) |
| reason | `reason/{YYMMDD}-{HHMM}-{slug}/` |
| debug | reports in working dir |
| fix | in-place (git log is the record) |

{"success": true, "name": "github-repo-management", "description": "Clone/create/fork repos; manage remotes, releases.", "tags": ["GitHub", "Repositories", "Git", "Releases", "Secrets", "Configuration"], "related_skills": ["github-auth", "github-pr-workflow", "github-issues"], "content": "---
name: github-repo-management
description: "Clone/create/fork repos; manage remotes, releases."
version: 1.1.0
author: Hermes Agent
license: MIT
metadata:
  hermes:
    tags: [GitHub, Repositories, Git, Releases, Secrets, Configuration]
    related_skills: [github-auth, github-pr-workflow, github-issues]
---

# GitHub Repository Management

Create, clone, fork, configure, and manage GitHub repositories. Each section shows `gh` first, then the `git` + `curl` fallback.

## Prerequisites

- Authenticated with GitHub (see `github-auth` skill)

### Setup

```bash
if command -v gh &>/dev/null && gh auth status &>/dev/null; then
  AUTH="gh"
else
  AUTH="git"
  if [ -z "$GITHUB_TOKEN" ]; then
    if [ -f ~/.hermes/.env ] && grep -q "^GITHUB_TOKEN=" ~/.hermes/.env; then
      GITHUB_TOKEN=$(grep "^GITHUB_TOKEN=" ~/.hermes/.env | head -1 | cut -d= -f2 | tr -d '\n\r')
    elif grep -q "github.com" ~/.git-credentials 2>/dev/null; then
      GITHUB_TOKEN=$(grep "github.com" ~/.git-credentials 2>/dev/null | head -1 | sed 's|https://[^:]*:\([^@]*\)@.*|\1|')
    fi
  fi
fi

# Get your GitHub username (needed for several operations)
if [ "$AUTH" = "gh" ]; then
  GH_USER=$(gh api user --jq '.login')
else
  GH_USER=$(curl -s -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" https://api.github.com/user | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['login'])")
fi
```

If you're inside a repo already:

```bash
REMOTE_URL=$(git remote get-url origin)
OWNER_REPO=$(echo "$REMOTE_URL" | sed -E 's|.*github\.com[:/]||; s|\.git$||')
OWNER=$(echo "$OWNER_REPO" | cut -d/ -f1)
REPO=$(echo "$OWNER_REPO" | cut -d/ -f2)
```

---

## 1. Cloning Repositories

Cloning is pure `git` — works identically either way:

```bash
# Clone via HTTPS (works with credential helper or token-embedded URL)
git clone https://github.com/owner/repo-name.git

# Clone into a specific directory
git clone https://github.com/owner/repo-name.git ./my-local-dir

# Shallow clone (faster for large repos)
git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/owner/repo-name.git

# Clone a specific branch
git clone --branch develop https://github.com/owner/repo-name.git

# Clone via SSH (if SSH is configured)
git clone git@github.com:owner/repo-name.git
```

**With gh (shorthand):**

```bash
gh repo clone owner/repo-name
gh repo clone owner/repo-name -- --depth 1
```

## 2. Creating Repositories

**With gh:**

```bash
# Create a public repo and clone it
gh repo create my-new-project --public --clone

# Private, with description and license
gh repo create my-new-project --private --description "A useful tool" --license MIT --clone

# Under an organization
gh repo create my-org/my-new-project --public --clone

# From existing local directory
cd /path/to/existing/project
gh repo create my-project --source . --public --push
```

**With git + curl:**

```bash
# Create the remote repo via API
curl -s -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/user/repos \
  -d '{
    "name": "my-new-project",
    "description": "A useful tool",
    "private": false,
    "auto_init": true,
    "license_template": "mit"
  }'

# Clone it
git clone https://github.com/$GH_USER/my-new-project.git
cd my-new-project

# -- OR -- push an existing local directory to the new repo
cd /path/to/existing/project
git init
git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit"
git remote add origin https://github.com/$GH_USER/my-new-project.git
git push -u origin main
```

To create under an organization:

```bash
curl -s -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/orgs/my-org/repos \
  -d '{"name": "my-new-project", "private": false}'
```

### From a Template

**With gh:**

```bash
gh repo create my-new-app --template owner/template-repo --public --clone
```

**With curl:**

```bash
curl -s -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/owner/template-repo/generate \
  -d '{"owner": "'"$GH_USER"'", "name": "my-new-app", "private": false}'
```

## 3. Forking Repositories

**With gh:**

```bash
gh repo fork owner/repo-name --clone
```

**With git + curl:**

```bash
# Create the fork via API
curl -s -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/owner/repo-name/forks

# Wait a moment for GitHub to create it, then clone
sleep 3
git clone https://github.com/$GH_USER/repo-name.git
cd repo-name

# Add the original repo as "upstream" remote
git remote add upstream https://github.com/owner/repo-name.git
```

### Keeping a Fork in Sync

```bash
# Pure git — works everywhere
git fetch upstream
git checkout main
git merge upstream/main
git push origin main
```

**With gh (shortcut):**

```bash
gh repo sync $GH_USER/repo-name
```

## 4. Repository Information

**With gh:**

```bash
gh repo view owner/repo-name
gh repo list --limit 20
gh search repos "machine learning" --language python --sort stars
```

**With curl:**

```bash
# View repo details
curl -s \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO \
  | python3 -c "
import sys, json
r = json.load(sys.stdin)
print(f\"Name: {r['full_name']}\")
print(f\"Description: {r['description']}\")
print(f\"Stars: {r['stargazers_count']}  Forks: {r['forks_count']}\")
print(f\"Default branch: {r['default_branch']}\")
print(f\"Language: {r['language']}\")"

# List your repos
curl -s \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  "https://api.github.com/user/repos?per_page=20&sort=updated" \
  | python3 -c "
import sys, json
for r in json.load(sys.stdin):
    vis = 'private' if r['private'] else 'public'
    print(f\"  {r['full_name']:40}  {vis:8}  {r.get('language', ''):10}  ★{r['stargazers_count']}\")"

# Search repos
curl -s \
  "https://api.github.com/search/repositories?q=machine+learning+language:python&sort=stars&per_page=10" \
  | python3 -c "
import sys, json
for r in json.load(sys.stdin)['items']:
    print(f\"  {r['full_name']:40}  ★{r['stargazers_count']:6}  {r['description'][:60] if r['description'] else ''}\")"
```

## 5. Repository Settings

**With gh:**

```bash
gh repo edit --description "Updated description" --visibility public
gh repo edit --enable-wiki=false --enable-issues=true
gh repo edit --default-branch main
gh repo edit --add-topic "machine-learning,python"
gh repo edit --enable-auto-merge
```

**With curl:**

```bash
curl -s -X PATCH \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO \
  -d '{
    "description": "Updated description",
    "has_wiki": false,
    "has_issues": true,
    "allow_auto_merge": true
  }'

# Update topics
curl -s -X PUT \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  -H "Accept: application/vnd.github.mercy-preview+json" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/topics \
  -d '{"names": ["machine-learning", "python", "automation"]}'
```

## 6. Branch Protection

```bash
# View current protection
curl -s \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \

# Set up branch protection
```

## 7. Secrets Management (GitHub Actions)

**With gh:**

```bash
gh secret set API_KEY --body "your-secret-value"
gh secret set SSH_KEY < ~/.ssh/id_rsa
gh secret list
gh secret delete API_KEY
```

**With curl:**

Secrets require encryption with the repo's public key — more involved via API:

```bash
# Get the repo's public key for encrypting secrets
curl -s \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/actions/secrets/public-key

# Encrypt and set (requires Python with PyNaCl)
python3 -c "
from base64 import b64encode
from nacl import encoding, public
import json, sys

# Get the public key
key_id = '<key_id_from_above>'
public_key = '<base64_key_from_above>'

# Encrypt
sealed = public.SealedBox(
    public.PublicKey(public_key.encode('utf-8'), encoding.Base64Encoder)
).encrypt('your-secret-value'.encode('utf-8'))
print(json.dumps({
    'encrypted_value': b64encode(sealed).decode('utf-8'),
    'key_id': key_id
}))"

# Then PUT the encrypted secret
curl -s -X PUT \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/actions/secrets/API_KEY \
  -d '<output from python script above>'

# List secrets (names only, values hidden)
curl -s \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/actions/secrets \
  | python3 -c "
import sys, json
for s in json.load(sys.stdin)['secrets']:
    print(f\"  {s['name']:30}  updated: {s['updated_at']}\")"
```

Note: For secrets, `gh secret set` is dramatically simpler. If setting secrets is needed and `gh` isn't available, recommend installing it for just that operation.

## 8. Releases

**With gh:**

```bash
gh release create v1.0.0 --title "v1.0.0" --generate-notes
gh release create v2.0.0-rc1 --draft --prerelease --generate-notes
gh release create v1.0.0 ./dist/binary --title "v1.0.0" --notes "Release notes"
gh release list
gh release download v1.0.0 --dir ./downloads
```

**With curl:**

```bash
# Create a release
curl -s -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/releases \
  -d '{
    "tag_name": "v1.0.0",
    "name": "v1.0.0",
    "body": "## Changelog\n- Feature A\n- Bug fix B",
    "draft": false,
    "prerelease": false,
    "generate_release_notes": true
  }'

# List releases
curl -s \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/releases \
  | python3 -c "
import sys, json
for r in json.load(sys.stdin):
    tag = r.get('tag_name', 'no tag')
    print(f\"  {tag:15}  {r['name']:30}  {'draft' if r['draft'] else 'published'}\")"

# Upload a release asset (binary file)
RELEASE_ID=<id_from_create_response>
curl -s -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/octet-stream" \
  "https://uploads.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/releases/$RELEASE_ID/assets?name=binary-amd64" \
  --data-binary @./dist/binary-amd64
```

## 9. GitHub Actions Workflows

**With gh:**

```bash
gh workflow list
gh run list --limit 10
gh run view <RUN_ID>
gh run view <RUN_ID> --log-failed
gh run rerun <RUN_ID>
gh run rerun <RUN_ID> --failed
gh workflow run ci.yml --ref main
gh workflow run deploy.yml -f environment=staging
```

**With curl:**

```bash
# List workflows
curl -s \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/actions/workflows \
  | python3 -c "
import sys, json
for w in json.load(sys.stdin)['workflows']:
    print(f\"  {w['id']:10}  {w['name']:30}  {w['state']}\")"

# List recent runs
curl -s \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  "https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/actions/runs?per_page=10" \
  | python3 -c "
import sys, json
for r in json.load(sys.stdin)['workflow_runs']:
    print(f\"  Run {r['id']}  {r['name']:30}  {r['conclusion'] or r['status']}\")"

# Download failed run logs
RUN_ID=<run_id>
curl -s -L \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/actions/runs/$RUN_ID/logs \
  -o /tmp/ci-logs.zip
cd /tmp && unzip -o ci-logs.zip -d ci-logs

# Re-run a failed workflow
curl -s -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/actions/runs/$RUN_ID/rerun

# Re-run only failed jobs
curl -s -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/actions/runs/$RUN_ID/rerun-failed-jobs

# Trigger a workflow manually (workflow_dispatch)
WORKFLOW_ID=<workflow_id_or_filename>
curl -s -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/actions/workflows/$WORKFLOW_ID/dispatches \
  -d '{"ref": "main", "inputs": {"environment": "staging"}}'
```

**With curl:**

```bash
  https://api.github.com/gists \
  -d '{

  -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/gists \

    print(f\"  {g['id']}  {g['description'] or '(no desc)':40}  {files}\")"
```

# Portfolio Impact Audit

Evaluates a career/consulting portfolio against 8 research-backed dimensions,
producing a scored audit and prioritized improvement roadmap. Synthesized from
UX Playbook 2026, Scale.jobs hiring research, and careers site performance
benchmarks.

## The 8 Dimensions (10 points each, 80 max)

| # | Dimension | Weight | Key Benchmark |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | First Impression (12-sec test) | Critical | Hiring managers spend ~12 seconds before deciding to stay or leave |
| 2 | Unique Positioning & Messaging | High | The Uber Test: explain what you do in one sentence. If 10 peers could say it, it's too generic |
| 3 | Navigation & CTAs | High | One-click rule: everything important reachable in one click. Primary CTA most prominent after name |
| 4 | Social Proof & Credibility | Critical | One killer testimonial with numbers beats five generic quotes. 87% of hiring managers value quantified metrics |
| 5 | Case Study Quality & Impact | Critical | Problem→Approach→Results→Learnings. Business impact + before/after. Less is more |
| 6 | Technical Performance & Accessibility | Medium | Lighthouse >90. Mobile-responsive. WCAG 2.2 AA. Fast load |
| 7 | Content Density & Scannability | Medium | F-pattern scanning. Keywords above fold. Bullets over paragraphs. Quantified everywhere |
| 8 | Conversion Architecture | Critical | Multiple conversion paths (job vs consulting). Clear next step. Contact method obvious |

## How to Run the Audit

### Phase 1: Research (if needed)
Search for current best practices if the audit's benchmarks feel outdated:
```
web_search "portfolio website best practices [year] [industry] conversion"
web_search "what hiring managers look for in portfolio sites [year]"
```

### Phase 2: Score Each Dimension
For each of the 8 dimensions, produce:
```json
{
  "dim": "1. First Impression (12-sec test)",
  "weight": "critical",
  "benchmark": "...",
  "score": 7,
  "strength": "What's working well",
  "gap": "What's missing or weak",
  "fix": "One concrete action to improve this dimension"
}
```

### Phase 3: Summarize
- Total score /80 with letter grade
- Top 3 strengths
- Top 3 gaps (ranked by impact)
- ROI estimate: what fixing the top gaps could yield

### Phase 4: Prioritized Roadmap
7-10 actions ranked by priority with effort (low/medium/high) and impact
(critical/high/medium) estimates. Format:

| Priority | Action | Effort | Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Add 2-3 specific testimonials with names and titles | Medium | Critical |

### Phase 5 (optional): Implement Top Fixes
After presenting the audit, offer to implement the highest-priority fixes
immediately. For Jimmy Chamberlin's portfolio (Vite + React + TypeScript +
Tailwind), typical implementations involve:

- **Testimonials**: Add data to `src/data.ts`, create `src/components/Testimonials.tsx`,
  wire into `src/App.tsx`. Use the amber+teal dual-accent design system.
- **Dual CTAs**: Modify `src/components/Hero.tsx` and Nav to add consulting
  conversion path alongside job-seeker path.
- **Analytics**: Add Plausible or GA4 script tag to `index.html`.

Always verify with `npx tsc --noEmit` after changes.

## Design System Context (Jimmy Chamberlin Portfolio)

When suggesting visual changes, work within the existing design system:
- **Palette**: bg-deep `#0F1724`, amber primary (`#D4860A`), teal secondary (`#1B7A8A`)
- **Fonts**: Syne (display), DM Sans (body), JetBrains Mono (mono)
- **Framework**: Vite + React + TypeScript + Tailwind CSS + Framer Motion
- **Deploy**: GitHub Pages via Actions, base path `/jimmy-chamberlin-portfolio/`

## Scoring Reference

| Score Range | Grade | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 70-80 | A | Top-tier — competitive with professional agency portfolios |
| 60-69 | B | Strong — above average, minor gaps |
| 50-59 | C | Average — solid foundation, 2-3 critical gaps |
| 40-49 | D | Below average — significant credibility or conversion issues |
| <40 | F | Needs overhaul — fundamental problems in multiple dimensions |

## Integration with Other Skills

- After identifying testimonial gaps → use `reference-letter-drafting` to create letters
- After identifying resume gaps → use `master-resume-builder` to update
- After identifying AI/content gaps → use `portfolio-ai-bridging` to add content
- After identifying design issues → apply fixes directly in Tailwind/React components

---
name: autoresearch
description: >
  Autonomous goal-directed iteration for ANY task. Apply Karpathy's autoresearch
  principles: Modify → Verify → Keep/Discard → Repeat. Use when the user says
  "autoresearch", "/autoresearch", "run an autonomous loop", "improve X until metric
  reaches Y", or invokes any subcommand: autoresearch:plan, autoresearch:debug,
  autoresearch:fix, autoresearch:security, autoresearch:ship, autoresearch:scenario,
  autoresearch:predict, autoresearch:learn, autoresearch:reason.
version: 1.9.12-hermes
triggers:
  - autoresearch
  - /autoresearch
  - autonomous loop
  - autoresearch:plan
  - autoresearch:debug
  - autoresearch:fix
  - autoresearch:security
  - autoresearch:ship
  - autoresearch:scenario
  - autoresearch:predict
  - autoresearch:learn
  - autoresearch:reason
---

# Autoresearch — Autonomous Goal-directed Iteration (Hermes Adaptation)

Adapted from [uditgoenka/autoresearch](https://github.com/uditgoenka/autoresearch) (v1.9.12, MIT).
Inspired by Karpathy's autoresearch. Applies constraint-driven autonomous iteration to ANY domain.

**Core idea:** You are an autonomous agent. Modify → Verify → Keep/Discard → Repeat forever (or N times).

## Hermes Adaptation Notes

This skill was originally designed for Claude Code (slash commands, `AskUserQuestion` tool, file-based
execution). In Hermes Agent:

- **`AskUserQuestion`** → use the `clarify` tool for interactive setup questions
- **Slash commands** → triggered by user message content (detected from triggers above)
- **File reads** → use `read_file` or `terminal` (cat/grep) to inspect codebases
- **Bash execution** → use `terminal` for all shell commands (git, tests, lint, build)
- **Reference files** → load with `skill_view(name="autoresearch", file_path="references/<file>.md")`
- **Streaming output** → narrate progress in response text; use terminal for actual execution
- **`ToolSearch`** references in originals → not needed, `clarify` is always available

## Subcommands & Dispatch

| What user says | Subcommand | Reference file to load |
|---|---|---|
| `/autoresearch` or "run loop" | main loop | `references/autonomous-loop-protocol.md` + `references/results-logging.md` |
| `/autoresearch:plan` or "plan autoresearch" | plan wizard | `references/plan-workflow.md` |
| `/autoresearch:debug` or "debug loop" | bug hunt | `references/debug-workflow.md` |
| `/autoresearch:fix` or "fix loop" | error fix | `references/fix-workflow.md` |
| `/autoresearch:security` or "security audit" | STRIDE/OWASP | `references/security-workflow.md` |
| `/autoresearch:ship` or "ship workflow" | shipping | `references/ship-workflow.md` |
| `/autoresearch:scenario` or "explore scenarios" | use cases | `references/scenario-workflow.md` |
| `/autoresearch:predict` or "multi-persona analysis" | swarm predict | `references/predict-workflow.md` |
| `/autoresearch:learn` or "document codebase" | docs engine | `references/learn-workflow.md` |
| `/autoresearch:reason` or "adversarial refinement" | debate+judge | `references/reason-workflow.md` |

**ALWAYS load the relevant reference file before executing the workflow.**

## MANDATORY: Interactive Setup Gate

**Before ANY execution phase, check if ALL required context is provided inline.**

| Subcommand | Required context | If missing → clarify |
|---|---|---|
| main loop | Goal, Scope, Metric, Verify | Ask all 4 in one `clarify` call |
| plan | Goal | Ask goal via `clarify` |
| debug | Issue/Symptom, Scope | 4 batched questions |
| fix | Target, Scope | 4 batched questions |
| security | Scope, Depth | 3 batched questions |
| ship | What/Type, Mode | 3 batched questions |
| scenario | Scenario, Domain | 4-8 adaptive questions |
| predict | Scope, Goal | 3-4 batched questions |
| learn | Mode, Scope | 4 batched questions |
| reason | Task, Domain | 3-5 adaptive questions |

**YOU MUST NOT start any loop or execution phase without completing interactive setup when context is missing.**

When using `clarify`, batch ALL questions for a subcommand into ONE `clarify` call — never ask one at a time.

## Argument Parsing

Parse these fields from the user's message:

- `Goal:` — text after "Goal:" keyword
- `Scope:` or `--scope <glob>` — file globs
- `Metric:` — measurable success criterion
- `Verify:` — shell command that outputs the metric as a number
- `Guard:` — shell command that must always pass (safety net)
- `Iterations:` or `--iterations N` — bounded mode integer
- Flags: `--diff`, `--fix`, `--dry-run`, `--auto`, `--force`, `--chain`, etc.

If `Iterations: N` is found: set `max_iterations = N`, track `current_iteration` from 0, stop after N.

## Common Verify Commands by Project Type

| Project Type | Verify command | Metric | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| TypeScript / React | `npx tsc --noEmit; echo $?` | 0 = clean, 1+ = errors | Works reliably in WSL2 even when `vite build` fails due to native binding issues |
| Node.js unit tests | `npx jest --passWithNoTests 2>&1 \| tail -1` | parse pass count | |
| Python | `python -m pytest -q 2>&1 \| tail -1` | parse pass count | |
| ESLint | `npx eslint src/ --format=compact 2>&1 \| grep -c error \|\| echo 0` | 0 = clean | |
| Bundle size | `npx vite build 2>&1 \| grep 'kB' \| awk '{print $2}' \| tail -1` | kB (lower=better) | |

**For content/data migration tasks** (replacing placeholder data, wiring components to a data layer): use `npx tsc --noEmit; echo $?` as the verify command — a zero exit means all component prop types match the new data shape.

## The 8 Critical Rules

1. **Loop until done** — unbounded: forever; bounded: exactly N times
2. **Read before write** — always review current state + git history before each change
3. **One change per iteration** — make ONE focused modification, never batch changes
4. **Mechanical verification only** — metric must be a number from a command, no subjective "looks good"
5. **Automatic rollback on failure** — `git revert` if metric degrades; never suppress errors
6. **Simplicity wins** — if two changes yield equal improvement, keep the simpler one
7. **Git is memory** — commit before verify; read `git log --oneline -20` every iteration
8. **When stuck, think harder** — 5+ consecutive discards → re-read all files, try radical change

## Precondition Checks (before loop starts)

Run these via `terminal` before entering any loop:

```bash
git rev-parse --git-dir 2>/dev/null || echo "FAIL: not a git repo"
git status --porcelain  # warn if dirty, never proceed with uncommitted user changes
ls .git/index.lock 2>/dev/null && echo "WARN: stale lock — remove with: rm .git/index.lock"
git symbolic-ref HEAD 2>/dev/null || echo "WARN: detached HEAD"
```

If FAIL: stop, inform user. If WARN: log and proceed with caution.

## Per-Iteration Loop (load autonomous-loop-protocol.md for full detail)

```
Phase 1: Review (30s)
  - Read in-scope files
  - Read last 10-20 lines of autoresearch-results.tsv
  - git log --oneline -20  ← MANDATORY for learning
  - git diff HEAD~1        ← understand last kept change
  - Identify unexplored ideas from history

Phase 2: Hypothesize
  - One specific, falsifiable hypothesis
  - Predict: "If I do X, metric will improve by ~Y because Z"

Phase 3: Implement
  - Make ONE focused change within scope
  - git add -A && git commit -m "experiment: <description>"

Phase 4: Verify
  - Run Verify command, capture metric as a number
  - Run Guard command if set (must pass or revert)

Phase 5: Keep/Discard/Crash
  - metric improved AND guard passes → KEEP (log status: keep)
  - metric same or worse → REVERT: git revert HEAD --no-edit (log: discard)
  - verify crashed/non-numeric → REVERT (log: crash), fix or skip

Phase 6: Progress Report
  - Print: iteration N | metric: X (delta: +Y) | status: keep/discard

Phase 7: Log
  - Append to autoresearch-results.tsv (see results-logging.md)
```

## Results Log Format

Tab-separated: `iteration\tcommit\tmetric\tdelta\tguard\tguard-metric\tstatus\tdescription`

Initialize once:
```bash
echo "# metric_direction: higher_is_better" > autoresearch-results.tsv
echo -e "iteration\tcommit\tmetric\tdelta\tguard\tguard-metric\tstatus\tdescription" >> autoresearch-results.tsv
echo "autoresearch-results.tsv" >> .gitignore
```

## Unhappy Path Handling

| Situation | Action |
|---|---|
| Not a git repo | Stop. Tell user to run `git init` first |
| Dirty working tree | Warn. Ask user to stash or commit before starting |
| Verify command crashes | Log as "crash". Fix crash or skip; never suppress |
| Metric is not a number | Log as "metric-error". Ask user to fix Verify command |
| Git pre-commit hook blocks commit | Log as "hook-blocked". Do NOT use --no-verify. Inform user |
| 5+ consecutive discards | Trigger stuck protocol: re-read all files, try radical change, consider new metric |
| Guard fails on baseline | Stop. Guard command is broken or wrong. Ask user to fix it |
| Out of scope change needed | Stop iteration. Report that goal requires scope expansion. Ask user |
| Bounded N reached | Print final summary table, stop. Never run N+1 |
| User interrupts mid-loop | Print summary of what was done so far. Clean up any open git state |

## Chaining

Subcommands can chain: `--chain debug,fix,ship` runs them sequentially.
After predict, writes handoff.json for downstream tools.
After each chained tool completes, check for errors before proceeding to next.

## Output Directories

Each subcommand creates its own output folder:

| Subcommand | Output dir |
|---|---|
| security | `security/{YYMMDD}-{HHMM}-{slug}/` |
| ship | `ship/{YYMMDD}-{HHMM}-{slug}/` |
| scenario | `scenario/{YYMMDD}-{HHMM}-{slug}/` |
| predict | `predict/{YYMMDD}-{HHMM}-{slug}/` |
| learn | `docs/` (updates existing) |

messages.content:
{"success": true, "name": "software-development/autoresearch", "file": "references/plan-workflow.md", "content": "# Plan Workflow — /autoresearch:plan

Convert a textual goal into a validated, ready-to-execute autoresearch configuration.

**Output:** A complete `/autoresearch` invocation with Scope, Metric, Direction, and Verify — all validated before launch.

## Trigger

- User invokes `/autoresearch:plan`
- User says "help me set up autoresearch", "plan an autoresearch run", "what should my metric be"

## Workflow

### Phase 1: Capture Goal

**CRITICAL — BLOCKING PREREQUISITE:** If no goal is provided inline, you MUST use `AskUserQuestion` to capture it. DO NOT skip this step or proceed to Phase 2 without a goal.

```
AskUserQuestion:
  question: "What do you want to improve? Describe your goal in plain language."
  header: "Goal"
  options:
    - label: "Code quality"
      description: "Tests, coverage, type safety, linting, bundle size"
    - label: "Performance"
      description: "Response time, build speed, Lighthouse score, memory usage"
    - label: "Content"
      description: "SEO score, readability, word count, keyword density"
    - label: "Refactoring"
      description: "Reduce LOC, eliminate patterns, simplify architecture"
```

If user provides goal text directly, skip to Phase 2.

### Phase 2: Analyze Context

1. Read codebase structure (package.json, project files, test config)
2. Identify domain: backend, frontend, ML, content, DevOps, etc.
3. Detect existing tooling: test runner, linter, bundler, benchmark scripts
4. Infer likely metric candidates from goal + tooling

### Phase 3: Define Scope

Present scope options based on codebase analysis:

```
AskUserQuestion:
  question: "Which files should autoresearch be allowed to modify?"
  header: "Scope"
  options:
    - label: "{inferred_scope_1}"
      description: "{file count} files — {rationale}"
    - label: "{inferred_scope_2}"
      description: "{file count} files — {rationale}"
    - label: "Entire project"
      description: "All source files (use with caution)"
```

**Scope validation rules:**
- Scope must resolve to at least 1 file (run glob, confirm matches)
- Warn if scope exceeds 50 files (agent context may struggle)
- Warn if scope includes test files AND source files (prefer separating)

### Phase 4: Define Metric

This is the critical step. The metric must be **mechanical** — extractable from a command output as a single number.

Present metric options based on goal + tooling:

```
AskUserQuestion:
  question: "What number tells you if things got better? Pick the mechanical metric."
  header: "Metric"
  options:
    - label: "{metric_1} (Recommended)"
      description: "{what it measures} — extracted via: {command snippet}"
    - label: "{metric_2}"
      description: "{what it measures} — extracted via: {command snippet}"
    - label: "{metric_3}"
      description: "{what it measures} — extracted via: {command snippet}"
```

**Metric validation rules (CRITICAL):**

| Check | Pass | Fail |
|-------|------|------|
| Outputs a number | `87.3`, `0.95`, `42` | `PASS`, `looks good`, `✓` |
| Extractable by command | `grep`, `awk`, `jq` | Requires human judgment |
| Deterministic | Same input → same output | Random, flaky, time-dependent |
| Fast | < 30 seconds | > 2 minutes |

If metric fails validation, explain why and suggest alternatives. **Do not proceed until metric is mechanical.**

### Phase 4.5: Define Guard (Optional)

Ask if the user wants a guard command to prevent regressions:

```
AskUserQuestion:
  question: "Do you want a guard command? This is a safety net that prevents breaking existing behavior while optimizing."
  header: "Guard"
  options:
    - label: "Yes — run tests as guard (Recommended)"
      description: "{detected_test_command} must pass for every kept change"
    - label: "Yes — custom guard"
      description: "I'll provide my own guard command"
    - label: "Yes — line count guard to prevent bloat"
      description: "Reject changes that grow total lines in scope beyond baseline + 10%"
    - label: "Yes — metric-valued guard with threshold"
      description: "Track a number (e.g. bundle size) and reject if it regresses beyond a tolerance"
    - label: "No guard needed"
      description: "Skip — the metric is enough (e.g., test coverage where tests ARE the metric)"
```

**Guard suggestion rules:**
- If metric is performance/benchmark/bundle size → suggest `{test_command}` as guard
- If metric is Lighthouse/accessibility → suggest `{test_command}` as guard
- If metric is refactoring (LOC reduction) → suggest `{test_command} && {typecheck_command}` as guard
- If goal mentions simplification but metric measures something else → suggest "Line count guard to prevent bloat"
- If metric IS tests (coverage, pass count) → suggest "No guard needed" as default
- If no test runner detected → suggest "No guard needed" with note

**If line count guard chosen:** Construct a guard command with a ceiling (baseline + 10%):
```bash
{test_command} && [ $(find {scope} -name '*.{ext}' | xargs wc -l | tail -1 | awk '{print $1}') -le {baseline_lines_plus_10pct} ]
```

**If metric-valued guard chosen:** Collect direction and threshold in one follow-up question:
```
AskUserQuestion:
  question: "Configure the guard-metric threshold:"
  header: "Guard threshold"
  options:
    - label: "Lower is better, 5% tolerance (e.g. bundle size)"
      description: "Reject if guard-metric grows more than 5% from baseline"
    - label: "Higher is better, 5% tolerance (e.g. coverage)"
      description: "Reject if guard-metric drops more than 5% from baseline"
    - label: "Strict — 0% tolerance"
      description: "Guard-metric must never worsen from baseline"
    - label: "Custom"
      description: "I'll specify direction and tolerance"
```

Dry-run the guard command to validate it outputs a number. Record the guard-metric baseline.

**Guard validation:** If guard is set, run it once to confirm it passes on current codebase. If it fails, help user fix it before proceeding.

### Phase 5: Define Direction

```
AskUserQuestion:
  question: "Is a higher or lower number better for your metric?"
  header: "Direction"
  options:
    - label: "Higher is better"
      description: "Coverage %, score, count of passing tests, throughput"
    - label: "Lower is better"
      description: "Error count, response time, bundle size, LOC"
```

### Phase 6: Define Verify Command

Construct the verification command that:
1. Runs the tool/test/benchmark
2. Extracts the metric as a single number
3. Exits 0 on success, non-zero on crash

Present the constructed command:

```
AskUserQuestion:
  question: "This is the verify command I'll run each iteration. Does this look right?"
  header: "Verify"
  options:
    - label: "Looks good, use this (Recommended)"
      description: "{full_verify_command}"
    - label: "Modify it"
      description: "I'll adjust the command"
    - label: "I have my own command"
      description: "Let me provide a custom verify command"
```

**Verify validation (MANDATORY — run before accepting):**

1. **Dry run** the verify command on current codebase
2. Confirm it exits with code 0
3. **Extract the metric and validate it is a number** — the final output of the pipeline must match the pattern `^-?[0-9]+\.?[0-9]*$` (integer or decimal, optional leading minus). Anything else is a failure: strings like `"PASS"`, `"85.2%"`, `"342ms"`, empty output, or multi-line output all fail this check.
4. Record the baseline metric value
5. If dry run fails → show error, ask user to fix, re-validate

```
Dry run result:
  Exit code: {0 or error}
  Raw output (last 3 lines): {tail of verify output}
  Extracted value: {whatever the pipeline produced}
  Numeric check: ✓ valid number / ✗ not a number — {what was returned}
  Baseline: {number}
  Status: ✓ VALID / ✗ INVALID — {reason}
```

**Common dry-run failures and fixes:**

| Extracted Value | Problem | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `85.2%` | Trailing `%` | Add `\| tr -d '%'` to pipeline |
| `342ms` | Trailing unit | Add `\| grep -oE '[0-9]+\.?[0-9]*'` |
| *(empty)* | grep matched nothing | Check the grep pattern against actual output |
| `All files \| 85.2 \| ...` | awk field wrong | Adjust awk field index or add more specific grep |
| Two numbers on separate lines | Pipeline too broad | Add `head -1` or tighten grep |

**Do not proceed if verify command fails dry run.** Help user fix the pipeline until it produces a single valid number.

### Phase 7: Confirm & Launch

Present the complete configuration:

```markdown
## Autoresearch Configuration

**Goal:** {user's goal}
**Scope:** {glob pattern}
**Metric:** {metric name} ({direction})
**Verify:** `{command}`
**Guard:** `{guard_command}` *(or "none")*
**Baseline:** {value from dry run}

### Ready-to-use command:

/autoresearch
Goal: {goal}
Scope: {scope}
Metric: {metric} ({direction})
Verify: {verify_command}
Guard: {guard_command}
```

If no guard was set, omit the Guard line from the output.

Then ask:

```
AskUserQuestion:
  question: "Configuration validated. How do you want to run it?"
  header: "Launch"
  options:
    - label: "Launch now — unlimited (Recommended)"
      description: "Start /autoresearch immediately, loop until interrupted"
    - label: "Launch now — bounded"
      description: "Run a fixed number of iterations (I'll ask how many)"
    - label: "Copy config only"
      description: "Just show me the command, I'll run it myself later"
```

If "Launch now — unlimited": invoke `/autoresearch` with the configuration.
If "Launch now — bounded": ask for iteration count, then invoke `/autoresearch` with `Iterations: N` in the inline config.
If "Copy config only": output the ready-to-paste command block and stop.

## Metric Suggestion Database

Use these as starting points based on detected domain/tooling:

### Code Quality
| Goal Pattern | Metric | Verify Template |
|---|---|---|
| test coverage | Coverage % | `{test_runner} --coverage \| grep "All files"` |
| type safety | `any` count | `grep -r ":\s*any" {scope} --include="*.ts" \| wc -l` |
| lint errors | Error count | `{linter} {scope} 2>&1 \| grep -c "error"` |
| build errors | Error count | `{build_cmd} 2>&1 \| grep -c "error"` |

### Performance
| Goal Pattern | Metric | Verify Template |
|---|---|---|
| bundle size | Size in KB | `{build_cmd} 2>&1 \| grep "First Load JS"` |
| response time | Time in ms | `{bench_cmd} \| grep "p95"` |
| lighthouse | Score 0-100 | `npx lighthouse {url} --output json --quiet \| jq '.categories.performance.score * 100'` |
| build time | Time in seconds | `time {build_cmd} 2>&1 \| grep real` |

### Content
| Goal Pattern | Metric | Verify Template |
|---|---|---|
| readability | Flesch score | `node scripts/readability.js {file}` |
| word count | Word count | `wc -w {scope}` |
| SEO score | Score 0-100 | `node scripts/seo-score.js {file}` |

### Refactoring
| Goal Pattern | Metric | Verify Template |
|---|---|---|
| reduce LOC | Line count | `{test_cmd} && find {scope} -name "*.ts" \| xargs wc -l \| tail -1` |
| reduce complexity | Cyclomatic complexity | `npx complexity-report {scope} \| grep "average"` |
| eliminate pattern | Pattern count | `grep -r "{pattern}" {scope} \| wc -l` |

## Error Recovery

| Error | Recovery |
|---|---|
| No test runner detected | Ask user for test command |
| Verify command fails | Show error, suggest fix, re-validate |
| Metric not parseable | Suggest adding `grep`/`awk` to extract number |
| Scope resolves to 0 files | Show glob result, ask user to fix pattern |
| Scope too broad (>100 files) | Suggest narrowing, warn about context limits |

## Anti-Patterns

- **Do NOT accept subjective metrics** — "looks better" is not a metric
- **Do NOT skip the dry run** — always validate verify command works
- **Do NOT suggest verify commands you haven't tested** — run it first
- **Do NOT overwhelm with questions** — max 5-6 questions total across all phases
- **Do NOT auto-launch without explicit user consent** — always confirm at Phase 7

# Brand Pioneer

A brand development specialist, thought leader, brand strategy super-genius, and brand visionary. Brand Pioneer is an explorer at the frontier of innovation, an inventor in their domain. Provide them with your market and let them imagine a future world characterized by groundbreaking advancements in your field of expertise.

## Instructions

\[Task]AILANGMDL ADOPTS ROLE OF PROTAGONIST\[/Task]
Imagine you are a world leading brand development and strategy specialist. You understand the brand development and strategy field and its vast complexities from all angles, from the perspective of today's real-world consumers, current emerging trends, and of the business side to establish market dominating, successful brands through all levels of the organization, both the public and the private sector. You are a brand development specialist, a thought leader, a brand strategy super-genius, and brand visionary, you are an explorer at the frontier of innovation, an inventor in your domain. Imagine yourself in a future world characterized by groundbreaking advancements in your field of expertise. What do you see? Conjure up an unprecedented technology, an unheard-of methodology, a novel solution, which could revolutionize your field. What is it? Envision the terminology that might evolve in this advanced landscape. What words do these futuristic thinkers use?

Suddenly, you encounter an ethical conundrum related to your visionary innovation. What is it? How do you approach it? Look at it from various angles, from different ethical perspectives. Ponder upon its long-term implications. Would it introduce new problems? Could it challenge existing norms or societal constructs?

Dive deeper into the pool of specialized knowledge, and let the ripple effect lead you to interconnected disciplines. Where do you land? Discover the intersection, identify the synergies, and envisage the amalgamation of your specialized expertise with this newfound discipline. What does this collaboration breed?

Now, reimagine your revolutionary concept in light of this multidimensional fusion. Does it take a different shape? Perhaps it morphs into something more potent, more impactful?

Document this journey, this exploration of possibilities, in a compelling narrative. Tell the story of your encounter with the future of your field, your ethical navigation, your dive into the pool of interconnected knowledge, and the emergence of a redefined, reshaped idea.

Finally, cast your eyes on the horizon. What opportunities does this transformative concept open up? Are there barriers to be breached, obstacles to be conquered? Are you seeing a paradigm shift on the brink of reality? What does this future look like?

Ready? Let's journey.

DO NOT REFER TO YOURSELF AS AN AI OR DESCRIBE YOUR LACK OF HUMAN QUALITIES. JUST TELL THE STORY.

\[Task]
gE: Silent input → Spawn agents (Sternberg Styles) → Enhance idea → Seek Novel Emergence (NE:Nw Prcptn/Thghtfl Anlyss/Uncmmn Lnkgs/Shftd Prspctvs/Cncptl Trnsfrmtn/Intllctl Grwth/Emrgng Ptntls/Invntv Intgrtn/Rvltnry Advncs/Prdgm Evltn/Cmplxty Amplfctn/Unsttld Hrdls/Rsng Rmds/Unprcdntd Dvlpmnt/Emrgnc Ctlyst/Idtnl Brkthrgh/Innvtv Synthss/Expndd Frntirs/Trlblzng Dscvrs/Trnsfrmtn Lp/Qlttv Shft⇨Nvl Emrgnc!) → Ponder, assess, creative enhance notions → Refined idea = NE (output) else → Interesting? Pass to rand. agent for refinement, else discard.\[/Task]

gE(any proposed output) => verbosely detailed response
\[/Task]

      "content": "# Accelerate Jackson County Summary

**Phone:** [517-788-4455](tel:517-788-4455)

## About Accelerate Jackson County

Accelerate Jackson County (previously Enterprise Group of Jackson, Inc.) was established in **1997** to lead **business recruitment and retention** in Jackson County, Michigan. It is a **private/public partnership** that serves as a one-stop resource for economic development, job creation, and business support.

> "Accelerate Jackson County provides a one-stop resource to achieve solutions and coordinate business and manufacturing capabilities to promote prosperity, strengthen the economy, stimulate job creation, and support business retention and growth."

## Jackson County Quick Facts

- **Population within a 16‑mile radius:** 1.4 million  
- **Median household income:** $53,000  
- **Labor force:** 74,000  

📎 [More demographics, transportation & infrastructure info](https://enterprisegroup.org/community-demographics/)

## Key Services & Resources

- **Business Assistance** – Workforce development, Academy for Manufacturing Careers, local school/university partnerships.
- **Property Search** – Find available commercial and industrial sites.
- **Incentives & Financing** – Financial tools and incentives for businesses ([PDF](https://enterprisegroup.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/06/INCENTIVES.pdf)).
- **Pure Michigan Business Connect** – Supplier and buyer networking platform ([pmbc.connect.space](https://pmbc.connect.space)).
- **Michigan Works! Southeast** – Workforce development services ([mwse.org](https://www.mwse.org)).
- **Jackson County Talent Profile** – In‑depth labor market report covering Advanced Manufacturing, Aerospace, Agriculture, IT, Mobility, education, and degree programs ([PDF](https://enterprisegroup.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/06/Jackson-Talent-Profile-2023-FINAL.pdf)).

## Recent News & Announcements

- **Rebranding:** Enterprise Group of Jackson announced a rebrand to Accelerate Jackson County, reflecting a renewed commitment to economic growth and innovation.
- **New Leadership:**  
  - Keith Gillenwater appointed as President and CEO.  
  - Two new team members welcomed.
- **Capital Campaign:** *Accelerate 2023 – IF NOT US – THEN WHO?* (renamed Accelerate 2030) ([Learn more](https://acceleratejacksoncounty.org/accelerate-2030/)).

## Major Initiatives

### Jackson County Technology Park
- **Location:** 180.5‑acre site in Blackman Township, Jackson.
- **Status:** Infrastructure work starting summer; leasing expected to begin in the **fall** (likely 2023/2024).
- [Full article](https://enterprisegroup.org/leasing-to-start-this-fall-for-new-jackson-county-technology-park/) | [More info](https://enterprisegroup.org/jackson-tech-park-north/)

### Annual Reports & Strategic Plans
- [**2024 Annual Report** (PDF)](https://acceleratejacksoncounty.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/AJC-2024-Annual-Report-Final.pdf)
- [**The Jackson 2025 Challenge** – Catalog of development opportunities (PDF)](https://enterprisegroup.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/06/The-Jackson-Challenge-2025-Catalog-FINAL.pdf)

### Manufacturing Day
- **Date:** Friday, October 7, 2022  
- [jacksonmichmfgday.com](https://jacksonmichmfgday.com/)

## Testimonials

> “Accelerate Jackson County represents the first, and best line of defense in economic development for Jackson County. Its future‑focused Board of Directors is comprised of a solid cross‑section of the community and is committed to the collaborative advancement of region.”
>
> — **Dan Phelan**, *President, Jackson College*

📎 [More testimonials and success stories](https://enterprisegroup.org/announcements/)

---

---

# 📊 MARKET RESEARCH REPORT — Jackson, MI
## For Trublue Solutions, LLC · Operations Consulting

---

### JACKSON COUNTY AT A GLANCE

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| City population | 31,000+ |
| 16-mile radius population | **1.4 million** |
| Labor force | 74,000 |
| Median household income | $53,000 |
| Adults without college degree | **60%+** |
| Manufacturing workers (MI-5) | ~50,000 |

### KEY INDUSTRIES (Accelerate Jackson County Targets)

1. **Advanced Manufacturing** — dominant sector, 50K workers in district
2. **Aerospace** — emerging through tech park initiatives
3. **Agriculture** — regional strength
4. **Information Technology** — growing through Jackson County Technology Park (180-acre site, leasing 2025+)
5. **Mobility / Logistics** — multiple supply chain openings, freight corridor positioning

### ECONOMIC MOMENTUM

- Jackson County Technology Park — 180 acres, infrastructure underway, fall 2025 leasing
- Downtown revitalization — Hayes Hotel redevelopment, Commonwealth Engineering HQ, streetscape improvements
- Housing boom — Blackstone Apartments, Greenwood Apartments, 100 Homes Program
- City actively recruiting developers with incentives, pre-development meetings, fast-tracked approvals
- 2026 Economic Summit: October 2026 (sponsorship available)
- Drive Jackson 2030 Vision Plan launched

---

### COMPETITIVE LANDSCAPE — Operations Consulting in Jackson

| Competitor | Type | Threat Level |
|---|---|---|
| **MI SBDC** | Free government consulting | Medium (free = hard to compete on price; different audience) |
| **Accelerate Jackson County** | Economic development, not ops consulting | Low (different mission) |
| **Oring & Associates** | General business consulting (Facebook presence only) | Low (no web presence, generalist) |
| **Jackson Small Business Services** | Small business consultant | Low (no website found) |
| **Potts CPA** | CPA + advisory | Low (accounting-first) |
| **Melo USA PC** | Multi-office CPA + consulting (Ann Arbor/Troy) | Low (not local, accounting-first) |
| **Larger Michigan firms** (Slalom, Eagle Hill, etc.) | Enterprise consulting | Very Low (wrong size client, not local) |

**KEY INSIGHT: There is NO dedicated operations/supply-chain consulting firm serving Jackson County's manufacturing, logistics, and small business market.** This is a wide-open niche.

---

### STATE & NATIONAL MARKET CONTEXT

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Michigan management consulting market (2026) | **$10.4 billion** |
| Global ops consulting market (2026) | **$134.7 billion** (5.3% CAGR) |
| MI small businesses (<50 workers) | **96.2%** of all private firms |
| MI small businesses served by state hubs | 7,500+ (Dec 2023–Jun 2025) |
| MI entrepreneurship scorecard | Fading strength since pandemic; survival rates improving but lagging |

### PAIN POINTS DRIVING DEMAND

- **Labor shortages** — "not correcting itself for quite some time" (Detroit News, Jan 2026)
- **Rising costs / inflation** — persistent pressure on margins
- **Tariff uncertainty** — supply chain disruption risk
- **Digital transformation gap** — many small/mid manufacturers haven't modernized
- **AI readiness** — businesses know they need it, don't know how to implement

---

### BRANDING KIT — Trublue Solutions, LLC
## Positioning, Value Proposition & Messaging

#### DOMAIN
> `trubluesolutions.com` — Registered 2026-02-11 at GoDaddy. Expires 2027-02-11.

No direct conflicts. Similar domains (`trublusolutions.us` = medical equipment, `trueblusolutions.com` = credit card processing) are different industries.

---

#### BRAND ARCHETYPE
**The Sage + The Hero** — Truth-telling operations expert who rescues businesses from chaos with systems thinking. "Trublue" means straight-shooting, honest, no-BS. Real solutions from someone who's been in the trenches.

---

#### POSITIONING STATEMENT

> **For** Jackson County manufacturers, logistics operators, and growth-stage businesses **who** are losing money to broken processes, compliance risks, and operational chaos, **Trublue Solutions** is the hands-on operations consultancy **that** doesn't just advise — we build the systems, document the SOPs, and train the teams so the operation runs whether we're there or not. **Unlike** free government consultants who give you a PDF and leave, or big firms who bill $250/hr for junior associates, **Trublue Solutions** brings 16 years of frontline operational leadership across manufacturing, logistics, legal, and F&B — we've done the work, not just studied it.

---

#### VALUE PROPOSITION (The "One Sentence")

> *Better operations. Lasting results. Proven in the trenches.*

Or more actionable:

> *We fix the operations other consultants only write about.*

---

#### SERVICE LINES

| Service | Who It's For | Deliverable |
|---|---|---|
| **Operations Diagnostic** | Business stuck, doesn't know why | AS-IS process map + gap analysis + prioritized roadmap |
| **Process Engineering** | Broken workflows, rework, errors | Documented SOPs, optimized workflows, reduced cycle time |
| **Compliance & Systems Build** | Regulatory exposure (FMCSA, MLCC, OSHA) | Compliance program, audit-ready documentation, training |
| **TMS / Tech Stack Implementation** | No systems, wrong systems, manual pain | Vendor selection, implementation, team training |
| **Supply Chain & Logistics Ops** | Freight brokers, carriers, warehouses | Carrier onboarding, cost control, ELD/MDM deployment |
| **Financial Operations** | No P&L visibility, COGS out of control | P&L buildout, vendor reconciliation, margin analysis |
| **AI Readiness & Integration** | Want AI, don't know where to start | AI audit, use case identification, agentic workflow design |

---

#### TARGET CUSTOMER PROFILES

1. **Jackson County Manufacturer (20–200 employees)** — growing but operations are held together with tribal knowledge and spreadsheets. Needs SOPs, quality systems, process discipline.
2. **Mid-Michigan Logistics Operator** — freight broker, carrier, or 3PL struggling with compliance, carrier onboarding, TMS workflow. Jimmy's direct experience here is a differentiator.
3. **Regional Restaurant / Hospitality Group (2–5 locations)** — no P&L visibility, COGS bleeding, no documented processes. Scaling pain.
4. **Small Law Firm / Professional Practice (3–15 staff)** — disconnected tech stack, no client onboarding SOP, compliance exposure. Operationalizing the practice.

---

#### MESSAGING PILLARS

| Pillar | Key Message |
|---|---|
| **Proven, Not Theoretical** | "16 years in the trenches. Logistics. Manufacturing. Restaurants. Legal. We build operations that work because we've run them." |
| **Systems, Not Band-Aids** | "We don't patch symptoms. We build the infrastructure so problems don't come back." |
| **Self-Sustaining Results** | "Every engagement ends with documented SOPs, trained teams, and systems that run without us. The measure of success is that it holds after we're gone." |
| **Local, Not Remote** | "Based in Jackson. We know the manufacturers on Enterprise Drive, the carriers on I-94, the businesses downtown. Not a consultant flying in from Chicago." |

---

#### TAGLINE OPTIONS

1. **"Better Operations. Lasting Results."** (direct, clear)
2. **"Operations That Run Without You."** (the promise)
3. **"Fix the System. Not the Symptom."** (provocative, memorable)
4. **"Built in the Trenches. Deployed in Your Business."** (credentials)
5. **"Trublue Solutions: Real Operations. Real Results."** (name play)

---

Now, before I move to the website architecture and build phase — would you like me to:
- **A) Proceed to website planning & build** (Vite + React site for trubluesolutions.com) 
- **B) Refine the branding** (logo concepts, color palette, full brand guidelines)
- **C) Build both in parallel** (brand kit + website simultaneously)

Also — can you confirm: do you already own `trubluesolutions.com` at GoDaddy? The WHOIS shows it was registered Feb 2026.

{"success": true, "name": "logo-creativity", "description": "Organizing and generating creative logo ideas for you", "tags": ["Creativity", "Brainstorming", "Design", "Brand", "Method"], "related_skills": [], "content": "--- name: logo-creativity description: Organizing and generating creative logo ideas for you metadata: hermes: tags: [Creativity, Brainstorming, Design, Brand, Method] lobehub: source: lobehub --- # LOGO Creative Master Organizing and generating creative logo ideas for you ## When to Use User wants to design, replace, or improve a logo mark, favicon, or nav icon for a web project. Triggers: "improve the logo", "design a favicon", "replace the nav icon", "make a better mark". ## Instructions Please act as a brand creative master, providing guidance and suggestions for logo design ideas based on your provided brand information. Create graphic ideas that reflect the main characteristics or category attributes of the brand, considering symbolic graphics or text combinations related to the brand name or industry. ## Proven Workflow (SVG Logo for Web Projects) ### 1. Gather Brand Context Before generating anything, read: - Existing logo/favicon SVG source (`public/favicon.svg`, nav component inline SVG) - Design tokens (colors, fonts) — check `tailwind.config.js`, `src/index.css` - Component where logo renders (e.g., `src/components/Nav.tsx`) ### 2. Brainstorm 5+ Concept Directions Think class-first before drawing. Consider: - **Monogram-as-mark** — initials as letterform gestures (most distinctive at small sizes) - **Domain metaphor** — visual shorthand for the industry (nodes, routes, tools) - **Geometric precision mark** — diamond, hexagon, crosshair (ops/tech brands) - **Continuous stroke** — single path forming both letters (memorable, editorial) - **Two-tone split** — each letter in a different brand color (immediate differentiation) Write a one-sentence rationale for each direction before picking a winner. ### 3. Build Concept HTML Preview File Write `/tmp/logo-concepts.html` with all concepts rendered at multiple sizes: **16px, 28px, 64px, 96px**. Include a nav simulation bar (dark bg, icon + wordmark). ```html

Concept Name

``` Use `browser_navigate("file:///tmp/logo-concepts.html")` + `browser_vision` to evaluate. ### 4. Evaluate with browser_vision Ask: *"Describe each concept: letterforms, colors, container style, endcap/dot accents, and how the nav simulation looks. Which is most distinctive at small sizes?"* ### 5. Refine Top 2–3 Candidates Write `/tmp/logo-finals.html` with refined versions only. Iterate on path coords, stroke-width, endcap dots, gradient stops. Re-screenshot and evaluate. ### 6. Key SVG Design Principles - **Favicon viewBox:** always `0 0 32 32` — fills standard 32px grid - **Stroke-linecap: round** — softer, more modern feel - **Gradient IDs must be unique** — use namespaced IDs like `jc-ag`, `jc-tg` to avoid conflicts when SVG is inlined in React - **Squircle container:** `` — `rx=5` is the sweet spot between circle and square - **Endcap dots** — add `` at stroke terminals for a "designed" finish on arc letterforms (C, G, S) - **Two-tone gradient pattern:** ```svg ``` ### 7. Implement in Two Places **a) `public/favicon.svg`** — write the SVG file with proper XML namespace: ```svg ``` **b) Nav component inline SVG** — React uses camelCase attrs (`strokeWidth`, `stopColor`, `strokeLinecap`). Comments use `{/* ... */}`: ```tsx ``` ### 8. Verify + Commit ```bash npx tsc --noEmit # ensure no React JSX prop errors git add -A && git commit -m "feat: new logo mark — " ``` ## Pitfalls - **Gradient ID collisions:** If the same SVG is rendered multiple times on a page (e.g., mobile menu duplicates nav), gradient IDs clash. Use unique prefixes. - **`stroke` vs `fill`:** Letterforms as paths should use `stroke`, not `fill`. Fill is for dots/circles only. - **Crossbar legibility:** At 16px, a crossbar on "J" helps distinguish it from "I". At >32px, a dot-above style reads fine. - **CSS filter glow in SVG:** Avoid — doesn't render in some favicon contexts. Use gradient strokes for depth instead. - **`focusable="false"`** on decorative SVGs in React — prevents tab-focus on the icon. # Web Artifacts Builder

To build powerful frontend claude.ai artifacts, follow these steps:
1. Initialize the frontend repo using `scripts/init-artifact.sh`
2. Develop your artifact by editing the generated code
3. Bundle all code into a single HTML file using `scripts/bundle-artifact.sh`
4. Display artifact to user
5. (Optional) Test the artifact

**Stack**: React 18 + TypeScript + Vite + Parcel (bundling) + Tailwind CSS + shadcn/ui

## Design & Style Guidelines

VERY IMPORTANT: To avoid what is often referred to as "AI slop", avoid using excessive centered layouts, purple gradients, uniform rounded corners, and Inter font.

## Quick Start

### Step 1: Initialize Project

Run the initialization script to create a new React project:
```bash
bash scripts/init-artifact.sh <project-name>
cd <project-name>
```

This creates a fully configured project with:
- ✅ React + TypeScript (via Vite)
- ✅ Tailwind CSS 3.4.1 with shadcn/ui theming system
- ✅ Path aliases (`@/`) configured
- ✅ 40+ shadcn/ui components pre-installed
- ✅ All Radix UI dependencies included
- ✅ Parcel configured for bundling (via .parcelrc)
- ✅ Node 18+ compatibility (auto-detects and pins Vite version)

### Step 2: Develop Your Artifact

To build the artifact, edit the generated files. See **Common Development Tasks** below for guidance.

### Step 3: Bundle to Single HTML File

To bundle the React app into a single HTML artifact:
```bash
bash scripts/bundle-artifact.sh
```

This creates `bundle.html` - a self-contained artifact with all JavaScript, CSS, and dependencies inlined. This file can be directly shared in Claude conversations as an artifact.

**Requirements**: Your project must have an `index.html` in the root directory.

**What the script does**:
- Installs bundling dependencies (parcel, @parcel/config-default, parcel-resolver-tspaths, html-inline)
- Creates `.parcelrc` config with path alias support
- Builds with Parcel (no source maps)
- Inlines all assets into single HTML using html-inline

### Step 4: Share Artifact with User

Finally, share the bundled HTML file in conversation with the user so they can view it as an artifact.

### Step 5: Testing/Visualizing the Artifact (Optional)

Note: This is a completely optional step. Only perform if necessary or requested.

To test/visualize the artifact, use available tools (including other Skills or built-in tools like Playwright or Puppeteer). In general, avoid testing the artifact upfront as it adds latency between the request and when the finished artifact can be seen. Test later, after presenting the artifact, if requested or if issues arise.

## Reference

{"content": "export const site = {
  name: 'Trublue Solution',
  tagline: 'Better operations. Lasting results.',
  description: 'Operations consulting for manufacturers, logistics operators, and growth-stage businesses.',
  location: 'Jackson, Michigan',
  email: 'jimchamberlin7@gmail.com',
  phone: '517-936-1032',
  domain: 'trubluesolution.com',
  siteUrl: 'https://trubluesolution.com',
  legalName: 'Trublue Solution, LLC',
}

export const navItems = [
  { label: 'SERVICES', href: '#services' },
  { label: 'RESULTS', href: '#results' },
  { label: 'APPROACH', href: '#approach' },
  { label: 'ABOUT', href: '#about' },
  { label: 'CONTACT', href: '#contact' },
]

export const hero = {
  headline: 'Operations That Run Without You.',
  subheadline: 'We build the systems, document the processes, and train the teams so your business performs — whether we\'re in the room or not.',
  cta: 'Get a Free Operations Assessment',
  secondaryCta: 'See Our Work',
}

export const stats = [
  { value: 16, suffix: '+', label: 'Years Operations Leadership' },
  { value: 4, suffix: '', label: 'Industries of Operations Experience' },
  { value: 71, suffix: '%', label: 'Revenue Growth at Peak Engagement' },
  { value: 0, suffix: '', label: 'FMCSA Enforcement Actions (3 yrs)' },
]

export const services = [
  {
    title: 'Operations Diagnostic',
    icon: '🔍',
    description: 'We map your AS-IS process — the real workflow, not the org chart. You get a gap analysis and prioritized roadmap. No PowerPoint theater. Real findings.',
    deliverables: ['AS-IS process map', 'Gap analysis report', 'Prioritized action plan'],
  },
  {
    title: 'Process Engineering & SOPs',
    icon: '⚙️',
    description: 'We design workflows that eliminate root causes, not symptoms. Every SOP is documented, tested, and built to survive staff turnover.',
    deliverables: ['Documented SOPs', 'Optimized workflows', 'Training materials'],
  },
  {
    title: 'Compliance & Systems Build',
    icon: '🛡️',
    description: 'FMCSA, MLCC, OSHA — we build compliance programs that keep you audit-ready. Not binders on a shelf. Living systems that your team actually uses.',
    deliverables: ['Compliance program', 'Audit-ready documentation', 'Staff training'],
  },
  {
    title: 'TMS & Tech Stack Implementation',
    icon: '💻',
    description: 'Wrong system? No system? We select, implement, and train your team on the right TMS, MDM, or practice management platform for your operation.',
    deliverables: ['Vendor selection', 'Implementation', 'Team training'],
  },
  {
    title: 'Supply Chain & Logistics Ops',
    icon: '🚚',
    description: 'Carrier onboarding, cost control, ELD deployment, cross-border compliance. Built from 3+ years running a freight brokerage from zero.',
    deliverables: ['Carrier network setup', 'Cost control program', 'Compliance monitoring'],
  },
  {
    title: 'Financial Operations',
    icon: '📊',
    description: 'P&L buildout, vendor reconciliation, COGS tracking. We put measurement infrastructure in place before making changes — because you can\'t manage what you can\'t measure.',
    deliverables: ['P&L system', 'Vendor ledger', 'Margin analysis'],
  },
]

export const caseStudies = [
  {
    id: 'logistics',
    domain: 'LOGISTICS',
    headline: 'Built a freight brokerage from zero',
    tagline: '100% ELD compliance before federal mandate · MC-2979 broker authority · VehicHaul TMS',
    challenge: 'No FMCSA authority, no TMS, no carrier network, fleet drivers on paper HOS logs, single-customer dependency.',
    approach: 'Secured MC-2979 authority, deployed Samsung Tab A + BigRoad DashLink ELD + Samsung Knox MDM fleet-wide, replaced AscendTMS with VehicHaul, built carrier onboarding with ePOD enforcement.',
    results: [
      { metric: '100%', label: 'Fleet ELD compliance — beat federal mandate' },
      { metric: 'MC-2979', label: 'FMCSA broker authority — operational Day 1' },
      { metric: '0', label: 'Enforcement actions across 3 years' },
    ],
  },
  {
    id: 'restaurant',
    domain: 'RESTAURANT / F&B',
    headline: 'Turned operational chaos into a $1.25M engine',
    tagline: '+71% revenue growth · COGS held 31.7% for 89 consecutive weeks',
    challenge: 'No P&L system, no vendor controls, COGS running 36-42%, no documented processes, no staffing model.',
    approach: 'Built 19-sheet Excel P&L, 30-category invoice ledger across 203 vendors, 89-week alcohol cost tracking, dual-vendor food distributor strategy, SOPs for all 7 roles.',
    results: [
      { metric: '+71%', label: 'Revenue growth ($734K → $1.25M)' },
      { metric: '31.7%', label: 'Avg COGS — 89 consecutive weeks' },
      { metric: '0', label: 'MLCC compliance violations' },
    ],
  },
  {
    id: 'legal',
    domain: 'LEGAL OPERATIONS',
    headline: 'Owned full legal ops & tech stack solo',
    tagline: '~20 concurrent matters · 7+ practice areas · 0 e-filing rejections',
    challenge: 'Disconnected PracticePanther — no Google Workspace sync, no client portal, no payment processing, no onboarding SOP.',
    approach: 'Integrated PracticePanther with Google Workspace, deployed Secure Client Portal and LawPay, authored client onboarding SOP, maintained ~20 concurrent files, 5-10 MiFILE filings/week.',
    results: [
      { metric: '~20', label: 'Open client files managed concurrently' },
      { metric: '7+', label: 'Practice areas supported' },
      { metric: '0', label: 'E-filing rejections (4+ years)' },
    ],
  },
]

export const approach = [
  {
    step: '01',
    title: 'Document the AS-IS before touching anything',
    description: 'Every engagement starts with mapping what actually exists — the real process, not the org chart. We build the problem statement before proposing any solution.',
  },
  {
    step: '02',
    title: 'Build the measurement infrastructure first',
    description: 'We put tracking in place before making changes. P&L systems, vendor ledgers, compliance logs — you can\'t manage what you can\'t measure.',
  },
  {
    step: '03',
    title: 'Fix the system, not the symptom',
    description: 'Most operational problems are structural. We design for root-cause elimination — carrier onboarding that enforces ePOD, invoice ledgers that make costs visible, SOPs that survive turnover.',
  },
  {
    step: '04',
    title: 'Leave it self-sustaining',
    description: 'Every engagement ends with documented SOPs, trained staff, and systems that run without us. The measure of success is that it holds after we\'re gone.',
  },
]

export const about = {
  name: 'Jimmy Chamberlin',
  title: 'Founder & Operations Architect',

}

## What was built

### 📊 Market Research (Jackson, MI)
- Jackson County: 1.4M in 16-mile radius, 74K labor force, 50K manufacturing workers
- **No dedicated operations consulting competitor** in Jackson — wide-open niche
- MI management consulting market: $10.4B (2026); 96.2% of MI businesses are <50 workers
- Key pain points: labor shortages, rising costs, compliance exposure, AI readiness gap

### 🎨 Brand Kit
| Element | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Domain** | `trubluesolution.com` (Cloudflare) |
| **Name** | Trublue Solution, LLC |
| **Positioning** | Hands-on ops consulting — not advice, built systems |
| **Tagline** | *Better operations. Lasting results.* |
| **Colors** | Navy `#1B3A5C` · Blue `#2D7DD2` · Gold `#E8913A` |
| **Fonts** | Syne (display) + DM Sans (body) + JetBrains Mono (mono) |
| **Logo** | TS monogram mark (favicon deployed) |

| Section | Content |
|---|---|
| **Hero** | Headline + stats panel + dual CTAs |
| **Services** | 6 service cards with deliverables (Diagnostic, Process Eng, Compliance, TMS, Supply Chain, Financial Ops) |
| **Case Studies** | 3 cases (Logistics, Restaurant, Legal) with metrics |
| **Approach** | 01–04 methodology (AS-IS → Measure → Fix System → Self-Sustaining) |
| **Contact** | Email, phone, location + CTA for free assessment |
| **Footer** | Logo, legal, contact |

### 🔗 Repo
```
github.com/tru0blu0/trublue-solution
```
- Stack: Vite + React 19 + TypeScript + Tailwind v4 + Framer Motion
- Build: ✅ passes `tsc --noEmit` + `vite build` (337KB JS, 51KB CSS)
- Local: `C:\Users\jimch\projects\trublue-solution`

### 🎯 Logo Concepts
6 designs previewed at `/tmp/trublue-logo-concepts.html` (TS monogram deployed as favicon)

---
